obligations.distinction: Recognize and eliminate one directly nested pair of empty veils before marks or rings introduce competing structure.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one bare paired-veil release establishes the complete boundary as the actionable unit.

obligations.distinction: Release two nested paired veils while discovering that either eligible pair may be taken first.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop at four veils; another nested pair would repeat the same ordering choice without a new distinction.

obligations.distinction: Erase one complete empty veil fragment from a negative field so that a familiar double cut remains.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after sibling fragments make complete-fragment selection visible without propositional content.

obligations.distinction: Remove a nested veil whose exact form is supported by an equal older veil in an ancestor field.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the first mark-free ancestor-supported removal separates deiteration authority from mere depth.

obligations.distinction: Remove an exact marked descendant fragment supported by the same marked fragment in an ancestor field.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one owner, one ancestor support, and one descendant echo isolate binder-preserving deiteration.

obligations.distinction: A suitable final record carries the explanation of selection, highlighting, and deselection; this explanation warrants no controls-only puzzle.
obligations.stoppingRule: Explain these controls on the first suitable puzzle and add no dedicated controls record.

obligations.distinction: A suitable final branching record carries the explanation of timeline rewind and branch replacement; this explanation warrants no timeline-only puzzle.
obligations.stoppingRule: Explain timeline behavior on a suitable branching problem and add no dedicated timeline record.

obligations.distinction: Contrast shallow targets at opposite polarities.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the shallow opposite-polarity contrast is established.

obligations.distinction: Determine nested polarity by parity where rings and cuts defeat local-depth guessing.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the first nested-parity case that establishes parity rather than local depth as authoritative.

obligations.distinction: Eliminate a direct double cut while preserving its content intact.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after direct content preservation is visible.

obligations.distinction: Distinguish a compound eligible double-cut pair from an obstructed annulus near-match.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound content and annular obstruction create the eligibility decision; further internal complexity adds no new decision.

obligations.distinction: Wrap an exact atomic selection in a double cut.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after exact atomic selection is required.

obligations.distinction: Wrap an exact compound selection in a double cut as one intact proposition.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound exact selection establishes whole-selection behavior.

obligations.distinction: Erase a compound semantic subgraph as the complete selected fragment.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound subgraph boundaries, rather than visual fragments, determine the erasure.

obligations.distinction: Construct and place atomic content in a legal target.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the atomic construction-and-placement decision is visible.

obligations.distinction: Construct a boundary-delimited branch whose internal P-and-Q conjunction remains intact as one inserted semantic unit.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop when atom-by-atom, partial, larger, and wrong-host constructions cannot replace the exact grouped branch.

obligations.distinction: Copy compound content from an ancestor region into a descendant region.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound ancestry makes source authority and destination relation visible.

obligations.distinction: Remove an exact peer duplicate.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after exact peer equality is sufficient and visible.

obligations.distinction: Remove a descendant occurrence supported by an equal ancestor occurrence.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after ancestry, rather than mere visual equality, determines support.

obligations.distinction: Choose the exact compound duplicate over a near match.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound exactness defeats near-match selection; further compound size is padding.

obligations.distinction: Recognize a compound retained proposition through irrelevant context.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after irrelevant context fails to obscure the retained compound proposition.

obligations.distinction: Project one atomic conjunct from a binary conjunction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the binary atomic component-selection decision is visible.

obligations.distinction: Project a grouped conjunct as one intact proposition.
obligations.stoppingRule: Further internal complexity adds no new selection decision.

obligations.distinction: Select the required target from a ternary conjunction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop at the first ternary target-selection case; additional arity is padding.

obligations.distinction: Inject an atomic proposition as one alternative of a binary disjunction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after binary atomic alternative construction is visible.

obligations.distinction: Place an intact cut-delimited P-and-Q proposition as one alternative, preserving its internal conjunction through the downstream branch match.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one binary grouped branch separates intact alternative construction from atomic placement.

obligations.distinction: Place the supplied proposition in the required alternative family of a ternary disjunction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop at the first ternary placement choice; additional alternatives are padding.

obligations.distinction: Recognize an atomic proposition repeated as the whole conjunction operand.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after atomic conjunction idempotence is established.

obligations.distinction: Recognize a compound proposition repeated whole across a conjunction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound whole-proposition equality is required.

obligations.distinction: Recognize an atomic proposition repeated as the whole disjunction operand.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after atomic disjunction idempotence is established.

obligations.distinction: Recognize a compound proposition repeated whole across a disjunction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound whole-proposition equality is required.

obligations.distinction: Recognize atomic conjunction operands exchanged under the same owner.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after atomic owner-preserving conjunction exchange is established.

obligations.distinction: Recognize compound conjunction operands exchanged under the same owner.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound whole-operand and owner preservation are both visible.

obligations.distinction: Recognize atomic disjunction operands exchanged under the same owner.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after atomic owner-preserving disjunction exchange is established.

obligations.distinction: Recognize compound disjunction operands exchanged under the same owner.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound whole-operand and owner preservation are both visible.

obligations.distinction: Recognize the minimal three-role conjunction under reassociation.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the minimal conjunction associativity relation is visible.

obligations.distinction: Recognize conjunction reassociation when one semantic role is compound.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one compound role establishes whole-role preservation.

obligations.distinction: Choose the correct conjunction reassociation site across multiple sites or a scope boundary.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the first genuine site or scope-boundary choice.

obligations.distinction: Recognize the minimal three-role disjunction under reassociation.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the minimal disjunction associativity relation is visible.

obligations.distinction: Recognize disjunction reassociation when one semantic role is compound.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one compound role establishes whole-role preservation.

obligations.distinction: Choose the correct disjunction reassociation site across multiple sites or a scope boundary.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the first genuine site or scope-boundary choice.

obligations.distinction: Compose an atomic two-link implication bridge.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the minimal two-link bridge is established.

obligations.distinction: Discriminate the compound middle proposition that actually joins two implication links.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound bridge matching defeats superficial endpoint matching.

obligations.distinction: Compose the first three-link implication chain.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop at the first three-link chain; longer saturated chains are padding.

obligations.distinction: Preserve an atomic required invariant through a connected composition.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after atomic invariant preservation is required.

obligations.distinction: Carry a compound invariant through the connected path.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after whole-compound invariant preservation is visible.

obligations.distinction: Lift an atomic paired transformation through conjunction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the atomic paired correspondence is established.

obligations.distinction: Lift a compound paired transformation while preserving correspondence between the pairs.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound correspondence, rather than formula size, determines the pairing.

obligations.distinction: Derive a common result from two supplied cases.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the binary common-result relation is established.

obligations.distinction: Choose a genuine grouped common result across the first ternary case family.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop at the first ternary case with grouped-result discrimination; further branches are padding.

obligations.distinction: Map one atomic branch of a disjunction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one-branch atomic mapping is established.

obligations.distinction: Map two compound branches while preserving branchwise correspondence.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound two-branch correspondence is required.

obligations.distinction: Perform the first ternary branchwise mapping generalization.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop at the first ternary branchwise generalization; additional branches are padding.

obligations.distinction: Distribute a conjunction over a disjunction in the base direction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after this base directional form is visible.

obligations.distinction: Factor a common conjunction component from a disjunction in the base reverse direction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after this base directional form is visible.

obligations.distinction: Distribute a disjunction over a conjunction in the base direction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after this base directional form is visible.

obligations.distinction: Factor a common disjunction component from a conjunction in the base reverse direction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after this base directional form is visible.

obligations.distinction: Factor a compound common conjunction component as one whole factor.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound common-conjunction equality is required.

obligations.distinction: Factor a compound common disjunction component as one whole factor.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound common-disjunction equality is required.

obligations.distinction: Recognize an atomic proposition repeated whole in conjunction-family absorption.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after atomic conjunction-family absorption is established.

obligations.distinction: Recognize a compound proposition repeated whole in conjunction-family absorption.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound whole-proposition equality is required.

obligations.distinction: Recognize an atomic proposition repeated whole in disjunction-family absorption.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after atomic disjunction-family absorption is established.

obligations.distinction: Recognize a compound proposition repeated whole in disjunction-family absorption.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after compound whole-proposition equality is required.

obligations.distinction: Negate and reverse atomic implication endpoints.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after atomic endpoint reversal is established.

obligations.distinction: Negate and reverse a compound implication endpoint as one whole proposition.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after whole-compound endpoint treatment is required.

obligations.distinction: Construct excluded middle for an atomic proposition.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the atomic form is established.

obligations.distinction: Construct excluded middle for a two-variable compound proposition treated as a whole.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after whole-compound treatment is established for two variables.

obligations.distinction: Construct excluded middle for the first three-variable compound proposition.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop before a fourth variable; larger compound forms add no new decision.

obligations.distinction: Recognize a direct atomic complement despite one coherent, visually intimidating irrelevant fragment.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one deliberately crowded example; further irrelevant-junk complements add no new decision.

obligations.distinction: Transform a negated conjunction into a disjunction of negated components.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after this base directional form is visible.

obligations.distinction: Transform a disjunction of negated components into a negated conjunction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after this base directional form is visible.

obligations.distinction: Transform a negated disjunction into a conjunction of negated components.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after this base directional form is visible.

obligations.distinction: Transform a conjunction of negated components into a negated disjunction.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after this base directional form is visible.

obligations.distinction: Apply a conjunction-family De Morgan relation with a compound component preserved as a whole.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one compound conjunction-family case.

obligations.distinction: Apply a disjunction-family De Morgan relation with a compound component preserved as a whole.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one compound disjunction-family case.

obligations.distinction: Generalize a De Morgan family across the first ternary connective.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one ternary family generalizer; additional arity is padding.

obligations.distinction: Use reductio to establish a compound desired conclusion treated as a whole.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after whole-compound conclusion handling is required.

obligations.distinction: Select the contradiction whose derivation is relevant to the reductio assumption.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after a competing contradiction makes assumption relevance decisive.

obligations.distinction: Recognize the atomic feedback proposition in Peirce's relation.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the atomic feedback dependency is established.

obligations.distinction: Recognize a compound feedback proposition repeated as one whole proposition.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after whole-compound feedback matching is required.

obligations.distinction: An isolated double-cut record contrasts an eligible pair with an obstructed near-match whose blocker actually belongs to the annulus.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after annulus ownership, rather than visual proximity, decides eligibility; add no combination-only record.

obligations.distinction: An isolated copy record contrasts equal-looking occurrences in ancestor, descendant, and sibling relations whose authority differs.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the ancestry relation changes iteration or deiteration authority; add no combination-only record.

obligations.distinction: Exchange or reassociation recognition determines the component projected or alternative constructed without treating structural recognition as a local rewrite.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after recognition changes the routing choice; never add a structural-rewrite path or a combination-only record.

obligations.distinction: A double-cut topology change creates or removes the ancestry relation required for iteration or deiteration.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one copy-license mutation.

obligations.distinction: A double cut creates an opposite-polarity annulus in which a useful insertion becomes legal.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one insertion-oriented workspace.

obligations.distinction: A double cut creates the exact descendant boundary that lets an ancestor source deiterate a complete compound occurrence as one unit.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one deiteration-oriented boundary workspace.

obligations.distinction: A removable occurrence or side condition is also the sole source for a later structural dependency.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one structural-source preservation case; retain an implication-bridge case only if it is diagrammatically distinct.

obligations.distinction: A grouped product relation hands off into a sum relation analogous to `(A ∧ B) → (B ∨ C)`.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one grouped handoff; larger arity is already covered in isolation.

obligations.distinction: A product result contains the exact component consumed by a later implication.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one product and one continuation form a genuine dependency.

obligations.distinction: A changing proposition crosses a serial chain while a side condition survives to a later consumer.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one preserved-context serial composition.

obligations.distinction: A serial composition requires discriminating the context needed by a later consumer from irrelevant context.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after the first distinct required-versus-irrelevant-context case.

obligations.distinction: Branch preparation enables convergence through a common-tail topology.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one common-tail topology.

obligations.distinction: Branch-local chains prepare distinct branches for convergence.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one branch-local-chain topology; additional branches or hops are padding.

obligations.distinction: Shared context is exposed across a branch boundary for branch-local consumers.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one expose topology.

obligations.distinction: Shared context is recollected after branch mapping.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one recollect topology.

obligations.distinction: Factoring or distribution in a conjunction field reveals an absorption or idempotence redex.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one conjunction-field topology.

obligations.distinction: Factoring or distribution in a disjunction field reveals an absorption or idempotence redex.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one disjunction-field topology.

obligations.distinction: De Morgan produces a product consumed by projection or conjunction lifting.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one product-producing problem.

obligations.distinction: De Morgan produces alternatives consumed by disjunction mapping or case analysis.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one sum-producing problem.

obligations.distinction: An alternating connective boundary requires both De Morgan and distribution in a CNF-shaped topology.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one CNF-shaped interaction.

obligations.distinction: An alternating connective boundary requires both De Morgan and distribution in a DNF-shaped topology.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one DNF-shaped interaction.

obligations.distinction: Contraposition turns an otherwise unusable implication into the missing composition bridge.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one reversed bridge.

obligations.distinction: No split is supplied; two downstream handlers identify the useful excluded-middle proposition that manufactures the cases.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one manufactured split; use compound structure only when the branches consume it.

obligations.distinction: Under the reductio assumption, a compound consequence conflicts with separately available negated components, with a meaningful competing source.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one compound contradiction with a meaningful competing source.

obligations.distinction: A branch-building relation combines factoring or distribution with excluded middle across a factor boundary.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one branch-building dual topology.

obligations.distinction: A product-collapsing relation combines factoring or distribution with excluded middle across a factor boundary.
obligations.stoppingRule: Stop after one product-collapsing dual topology.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Two directly nested empty veils form one eligible double cut with no marks, rings, or competing content.
puzzles.defeats: A first proof action does not require decoding ownership or proposition marks; the complete paired boundary is itself actionable.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Four directly nested empty veils contain two eligible pairs whose release order is open.
puzzles.defeats: Repeating a familiar release does not impose one mandatory inner-first or outer-first route.

puzzles.visibleSituation: One dark field contains two complete empty veil fragments; removing either leaves the familiar paired form.
puzzles.defeats: Erasure acts on a complete fragment in a negative field rather than on an arbitrary piece of a boundary.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A complete inner veil repeats an equal surrounding veil, making supported removal visible before familiar cleanup.
puzzles.defeats: Deiteration depends on an equal supporting occurrence, not merely on visual depth or emptiness.

puzzles.visibleSituation: One globally bound mark appears in an ancestor field and repeats in a descendant field. This first content-bearing problem also provides live semantic targets on which selection, highlighting, and deselection can be explained without interrupting play; terminal binder cleanup follows only after the propositional work is complete.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that only peer duplicates authorize removal: an equal ancestor occurrence supports a descendant. It also defeats a controls-only exercise by attaching the interaction explanation to a real ancestry choice.

puzzles.visibleSituation: In `((A∧X)∨(B∧C)) → ((A∨B)∧(A∨C))`, X obstructs exact support in the negative A source while the corresponding positive A target refuses the same erasure.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats edit-by-proximity and edit-free copying: X must be erased in the legal negative field before the copied A branch can support the continuation.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A supported negative-P echo blocks Q inside one implication; removing that exact marked fragment exposes Q, which then feeds a separate Q-to-R continuation and denied R.
puzzles.defeats: The marked deiteration is not an isolated identity cleanup: it creates the only Q source used by the differently shaped downstream route.

puzzles.visibleSituation: An R-gated blocked target needs the exact shallow negative-P source to contain Q as well; inserting Q completes that source without disturbing the retained R context.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats indiscriminate insertion: only Q in the shallow source creates the exact marked fragment that can discharge the target.

puzzles.visibleSituation: The intact implication `(P∧S)→(Q∧R)` must be copied into descendant branches that separately continue to Q and R.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats atom-by-atom or partial-region copying: only the complete cut-delimited implication has ancestor-to-descendant copy authority.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Within `(R ∧ (P ∨ (Q ∧ R))) → (P ∨ Q)`, wrapping exactly P creates the double-cut occurrence consumed by the guarded continuation.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats selecting by proximity or extent: wrapping Q, P together with Q, or skipping the wrap cannot feed the same continuation.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 2 semantic owners, 11 owned atomic occurrences, and 6 cuts. Choose the exact compound duplicate over a near match.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that shared atoms or a similar silhouette are enough; the removable compound must match exactly.

puzzles.visibleSituation: The direct closed theorem (A∧B)∨(A∧C) → A∧(B∨C) presents the shared A inside both source alternatives and asks for one factored A with the residual B/C choice preserved.
puzzles.defeats: The common conjunction component must be recovered from both alternatives while the residual alternatives remain grouped in the conclusion.

puzzles.visibleSituation: (A∨B)∧(A∨C) exposes atomic A as the exact common alternative to factor into A∨(B∧C).
puzzles.defeats: A must be matched as the common alternative in both clauses; no separately available A can bypass the factoring relation.

puzzles.visibleSituation: The whole common alternative K=(P∨Q) occurs in both clauses ((K∨R)∧(K∨S)) and must remain intact in K∨(R∧S).
puzzles.defeats: Matching only P, only Q, or the silhouette of a partial alternative does not establish equality of the complete common disjunction.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A P-and-Q support faces a deeper P-Q-P field; wrapping exactly the intact P-and-Q compound creates an inner cut that matches the support.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats atom-first and select-everything guesses: wrapping P alone or the larger P-Q-P fragment cannot make the introduced inner cut removable, so the internal P-and-Q grouping remains decisive after the wrap.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Two P-and-Q double cuts share one workspace: the eligible pair has an empty annulus, while its near-match carries P in the annulus. Eliminating the eligible pair promotes its P and Q beside a pre-existing P.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats the silhouette rule and disposable-content reading: the obstructed pair cannot eliminate, while the selected pair preserves stable P-and-Q content that forms and discharges the exact P-P-Q target.

puzzles.visibleSituation: The direct closed theorem A∨(B∧C) → (A∨B)∧(A∨C) asks the same A alternative to survive in both conjunctive conclusions while B and C remain distinct.
puzzles.defeats: The shared alternative must participate in both conjunctive outputs, not only one.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 3 semantic owners, 7 owned atomic occurrences, and 8 cuts. Distribute a conjunction over a disjunction in the base direction.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes the base conjunction-over-disjunction direction; there is no prior narrower directional hypothesis.

puzzles.visibleSituation: One ownership ring carries four identical P marks through three nested veils, placing successive occurrences in alternating negative and positive hosts without any ring changing parity.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns local-depth and ring-color guesses: only the complete cut path gives polarity, and both the outer P side and the nested alternating side are required for the closed theorem.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Six global proposition owners form two asymmetric branches sharing the intact P-or-Q factor; exposing the factored result supplies the U continuation.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns both atomic-only factoring and endpoint factoring: the common factor is compound, and the factored whole is consumed downstream rather than merely opposed by an existing copy.

puzzles.visibleSituation: From P and P→(Q∧S), the compound intermediate supplies Q to Q→R while the irrelevant S component remains outside the continuation.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats treating product extraction as terminal: Q is extracted from an intermediate result specifically to feed the later implication to R.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 1 semantic owner, 3 owned atomic occurrences, and 2 cuts. Recognize an atomic proposition repeated as the whole conjunction operand. Remove an exact peer duplicate.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes conjunction whole-operand repetition; there is no prior narrower repetition hypothesis. This establishes exact peer-duplicate removal; there is no prior narrower equality hypothesis.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A complete compound proposition must remain intact while a separate irrelevant compound semantic subgraph is erased as one whole fragment.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats the hypothesis that weakening may nibble at internal atoms: the irrelevant disjunctive component is removed whole while the required disjunction remains intact.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A tiny empty veil and the densely grouped implication (P∧Q)→(R∨S) share one dark host; its two antecedent marks and two denied alternatives still form one disposable sibling.
puzzles.defeats: Unlike forked-veil's tiny disposable veil, this later tablet defeats the urge to work inward through an intimidating proposition: the sufficient empty veil makes the entire compound root erasable in one selection.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 2 semantic owners, 3 owned atomic occurrences, and 2 cuts. Project one atomic conjunct from a binary conjunction.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes selection of one atomic component from a binary product; there is no prior narrower component-choice hypothesis.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A grouped conjunct must be projected intact as one proposition instead of selecting one of its internal atoms.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats the hypothesis that projection always selects an atomic mark; the selected component is the intact Q-or-R proposition.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Exactly three product components offer distinct targets and only one feeds the goal.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats the binary left/right model of projection: three peer conjuncts are available and the goal owner singles out the middle one.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 2 semantic owners, 3 owned atomic occurrences, and 5 cuts. Inject an atomic proposition as one alternative of a binary disjunction.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes construction of one atomic alternative beside a supplied atom; there is no prior narrower placement hypothesis.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Within an R-and-not-T context, the shallow T source is completed by inserting one cut-delimited not-(P-and-Q) fragment, after which it exactly supports the blocked target.
puzzles.defeats: Loose atoms, P alone, a larger group, or the wrong host do not create the required whole occurrence; the cut boundary and both internal marks must be inserted together.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 3 semantic owners, 5 owned atomic occurrences, and 6 cuts. Place the supplied proposition in the required alternative family of a ternary disjunction.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that alternative construction is only a binary left/right choice; one target must be placed in a three-way family.

puzzles.visibleSituation: The same nontrivial compound proposition repeats whole across a conjunction, while partial internal matches do not count.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats partial-overlap matching: the repeated operand is the complete Q-or-R proposition, not either internal branch.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 1 semantic owner, 3 owned atomic occurrences, and 5 cuts. Recognize an atomic proposition repeated as the whole disjunction operand.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes disjunction whole-operand repetition; there is no prior narrower repetition hypothesis.

puzzles.visibleSituation: The same nontrivial compound proposition repeats whole across a disjunction, while partial internal matches do not count.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats partial-overlap matching: the repeated alternative is the complete Q-and-R proposition, not either internal conjunct.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Two atomic conjunction operands are exchanged while remaining under the same semantic owner.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats the hypothesis that left-to-right drawing order changes a conjunction or exchanges the propositions between their semantic owners.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A compound conjunction operand is exchanged whole with another operand while every semantic owner remains fixed.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats the hypothesis that only atomic operands can exchange visually; the complete opposite-connective operand keeps its owner and boundary.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 2 semantic owners, 4 owned atomic occurrences, and 8 cuts. Recognize atomic disjunction operands exchanged under the same owner.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes owner-preserving atomic disjunction exchange recognition; there is no prior narrower operand hypothesis.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A source disjunction contains P and the intact compound branch Q∧R. The target shows those same branches exchanged inside a deeper field beside unrelated X. Wrapping exactly the two exchanged branches creates the missing disjunction boundary, which the source can discharge.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats atomwise and oversized wrapping: only the two complete disjunction branches, with Q∧R preserved as one branch, acquire the source's boundary.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 3 semantic owners, 6 owned atomic occurrences, and 2 cuts. Recognize the minimal three-role conjunction under reassociation.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the two-operand hypothesis for conjunction recognition by making three semantic roles participate in the same flat owner.

puzzles.visibleSituation: The flat source P, Q, and R-or-S must be matched as one three-role conjunction to a consumer leading through T to U; the opposite-connective role remains intact.
puzzles.defeats: Projecting only R-or-S or selecting loose internal atoms cannot start the continuation: the full flat conjunction scope is the usable occurrence.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 3 semantic owners, 6 owned atomic occurrences, and 10 cuts. Recognize the minimal three-role disjunction under reassociation.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the two-alternative hypothesis for disjunction recognition by making three semantic roles participate in the same flat owner.

puzzles.visibleSituation: The intact marked negative-(P-and-Q) fragment is the only source that can enter the A-to-R link and then continue through R-to-S.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats routing from a visually nearby atom or breaking the marked source apart: the whole recognized fragment must survive to start the continuation.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Among multiple disjunction sites, one visibly compound opposite-connective proposition must remain one semantic role, and that role identifies the correct scope boundary.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats imagined binary grouping: the opposite-connective R-and-S branch remains one role and identifies the useful branch among three flat alternatives.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 3 semantic owners, 6 owned atomic occurrences, and 6 cuts. Compose an atomic two-link implication bridge.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes exact atomic middle matching across two links; there is no prior narrower chain hypothesis.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 4 semantic owners, 8 owned atomic occurrences, and 8 cuts. Compose the first three-link implication chain.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that serial composition stops after one two-link handoff; a first third link consumes the intermediate result.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 5 semantic owners, 10 owned atomic occurrences, and 6 cuts. Discriminate the compound middle proposition that actually joins two implication links.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that composition may match only an atomic bridge rather than one exact compound middle.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 4 semantic owners, 8 owned atomic occurrences, and 6 cuts. Preserve an atomic required invariant through a connected composition.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the strategy of following only the changing bridge: the invariant side condition is required across the connected composition.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 4 semantic owners, 8 owned atomic occurrences, and 6 cuts. Lift an atomic paired transformation through conjunction.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes paired atomic correspondence; there is no prior narrower correspondence hypothesis.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 6 semantic owners, 12 owned atomic occurrences, and 6 cuts. Lift a compound paired transformation while preserving correspondence between the pairs.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that paired lifting can track only atomic components.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 3 semantic owners, 7 owned atomic occurrences, and 9 cuts. Derive a common result from two supplied cases. Its genuine two-case proof also provides the first worthwhile branch on which rewind and branch replacement can be explained.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes that two supplied alternatives may converge on one common result; there is no narrower branch-count hypothesis. It defeats a timeline-only record by making the replaced branch an actual proof choice.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 5 semantic owners, 14 owned atomic occurrences, and 12 cuts. Choose a genuine grouped common result across the first ternary case family.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that case convergence must remain binary or produce only an atomic result.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 3 semantic owners, 6 owned atomic occurrences, and 10 cuts. Map one atomic branch of a disjunction.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes the base one-branch mapping relation; there is no prior narrower branch hypothesis.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 6 semantic owners, 12 owned atomic occurrences, and 10 cuts. Map two compound branches while preserving branchwise correspondence.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that branchwise mapping works only for one atomic branch or may ignore branch correspondence.

puzzles.visibleSituation: P∨Q∨R is paired with P→S, Q→T, and R→U, requiring three corresponding output alternatives S∨T∨U.
puzzles.defeats: Binary position guessing does not generalize: each of three source branches must be matched to its own result by correspondence.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 2 semantic owners, 4 owned atomic occurrences, and 5 cuts. Recognize an atomic proposition repeated whole in conjunction-family absorption.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes the base conjunction-absorption relation; there is no prior narrower operand hypothesis to overturn.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 4 semantic owners, 16 owned atomic occurrences, and 28 cuts. Recognize a compound proposition repeated whole in conjunction-family absorption.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that absorption works only when the repeated whole is atomic.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 2 semantic owners, 4 owned atomic occurrences, and 5 cuts. Recognize an atomic proposition repeated whole in disjunction-family absorption.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes the base disjunction-absorption relation; there is no prior narrower operand hypothesis to overturn.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 3 semantic owners, 7 owned atomic occurrences, and 5 cuts. Recognize a compound proposition repeated whole in disjunction-family absorption.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that disjunction absorption works only when the repeated whole is atomic.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 2 semantic owners, 4 owned atomic occurrences, and 8 cuts. Negate and reverse atomic implication endpoints.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes reversal with negated atomic endpoints; there is no prior narrower endpoint-shape hypothesis.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A contraposition problem reverses an implication while preserving its conjunctive endpoint as one whole proposition.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that contraposition may negate and reverse only atomic endpoints rather than a compound endpoint as one whole.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Excluded middle is constructed for one atomic proposition with no compound operand to obscure the base pattern.
puzzles.defeats: The base pattern establishes that one proposition and its exact negation exhaust the alternatives.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 2 semantic owners, 4 owned atomic occurrences, and 6 cuts. Construct excluded middle for a two-variable compound proposition treated as a whole.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that excluded middle recognizes only a single atomic proposition rather than a compound proposition as one whole.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 3 semantic owners, 6 owned atomic occurrences, and 6 cuts. Construct excluded middle for the first three-variable compound proposition.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that compound excluded-middle matching stops at two variables; the whole three-variable proposition is repeated.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A negated disjunction whose two operands are atomic expands into the conjunction of their negations, with no compound operand obscuring the base direction.
puzzles.defeats: Defeats: Negated disjunction has no simple atomic expansion pattern.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A conjunction of two negated atomic operands factors into their negated disjunction, with no compound operand obscuring the base reverse direction.
puzzles.defeats: Defeats: Factoring requires a compound operand before the direction is recognizable.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A negated atomic conjunction faces a denied disjunction of the two negations. Each source atom must acquire its own double veil before the completed transformed whole can discharge the denial.
puzzles.defeats: The start is not an identity: neither atomic branch alone produces the disjunction of negations, and the whole forward transformation is consumed after construction.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A negated three-role conjunction faces a denied three-way disjunction of the separate negations. All three source atoms must be transformed before the whole target exists.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the binary-only interpretation without adding a fourth padding role: omitting any one of the three peer transformations leaves the denial unmatched.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Opening a negated disjunction produces a negated compound P-and-Q branch and a negated atomic R branch; the two results separately feed S and T before those outputs meet a denied S-and-T goal.
puzzles.defeats: The compound branch cannot be split into incidental atoms and the other branch cannot be erased as junk: each transformed output supplies a different downstream consumer.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A disjunction of separately negated atoms faces a denied negated conjunction. Opening both double-veiled source branches constructs the whole reverse De Morgan form used to discharge the denial.
puzzles.defeats: The reverse direction is a construction rather than a pre-existing identity: both separated branches must contribute before the negated conjunction can be consumed.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A negated conjunction has a disjunctive P-or-Q role beside atomic R. The compound role is wrapped as one unit and R separately before the completed disjunction of negations can be consumed.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats atom-only transformation and partial compound matching: both the intact opposite-connective role and the atomic peer are necessary parts of the transformed whole.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A direct P/not-P complement sits beside a large but coherent transitive-chain fragment that can be removed as one irrelevant unit.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats the assumption that the most elaborate connected fragment must contain the key: the simple complement remains decisive despite intimidating irrelevant structure.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 3 semantic owners, 5 owned atomic occurrences, and 6 cuts. Recognize the atomic feedback proposition in Peirce's relation.
puzzles.defeats: This establishes atomic feedback matching; there is no prior narrower repeated-proposition hypothesis.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 3 semantic owners, 7 owned atomic occurrences, and 6 cuts. Recognize a compound feedback proposition repeated as one whole proposition.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that Peirce feedback may repeat only an atom rather than one compound proposition as a whole.

puzzles.visibleSituation: P, the required whole context S∧T, and the near-match S∧U sit beside a P∧(S∧T) to Q∧(S∧T) to R∧(S∧T) chain.
puzzles.defeats: Shared atoms are not enough: the complete S∧T context, rather than the similar S∧U context, is the invariant consumed at both handoffs.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Under denial of a compound conclusion, one structured contradiction depends on that denial while a competing contradiction is visible but cannot discharge it.
puzzles.defeats: Defeats: Any visible contradiction can discharge any denied conclusion regardless of scope or dependency.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Weakening removes spare R from an enclosed P-and-Q-and-R source, creating the exact whole P-and-Q needed by one branch of an alternative consumer; its T result then feeds T-to-U.
puzzles.defeats: Weakening and injection are not adjacent decorations: the whole source is unusable before weakening, the spare alternative cannot replace it, and the selected branch supplies a differently shaped downstream continuation.

puzzles.visibleSituation: One supplied implication becomes the exact missing middle bridge only after contrapositive reversal, enabling composition with a second link.
puzzles.defeats: Defeats: Composition can use the supplied implication without reversing and negating its endpoints.

puzzles.visibleSituation: No split is supplied; downstream handlers for a proposition and its negation identify the useful excluded-middle proposition, and both manufactured cases are consumed.
puzzles.defeats: Defeats: Case analysis always begins with a supplied split.

puzzles.visibleSituation: An alternating connective boundary requires both De Morgan and distribution to produce a recognizably CNF-shaped intermediate consumed downstream.
puzzles.defeats: Defeats: De Morgan and distribution can be applied independently without the produced intermediate constraining the next move.

puzzles.visibleSituation: An alternating connective boundary requires both De Morgan and distribution to produce a recognizably DNF-shaped intermediate consumed downstream.
puzzles.defeats: Defeats: The dual alternating boundary adds a mechanically identical extra clause rather than a distinct intermediate shape.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 6 semantic owners, 19 owned atomic occurrences, and 13 cuts. Shared context is exposed across a branch boundary for branch-local consumers.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the hypothesis that a branch boundary isolates each handler from globally shared context; the branch-local consumers require that exposed context.

puzzles.visibleSituation: P and Q prepare locally to R and S, converge separately to T, and only then share the single T→U continuation.
puzzles.defeats: The common tail cannot be applied before the branch-specific preparations establish its shared T input.

puzzles.visibleSituation: The P branch follows P→R→T while the Q branch follows Q→S→T before the cases converge.
puzzles.defeats: A single shared implication cannot stand in for the two different local intermediate states.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A manufactured P/not-P split creates two branches whose construction crosses a factor boundary and consumes the same shared factor in both branches.
puzzles.defeats: The shared consensus factor is not another independent case: preserving and using it changes how both classical branches must be built.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A classical split and dual distribution expose redundant product structure whose shared term changes the available collapse.
puzzles.defeats: Defeats: The third product factor is never controlled by a classical split.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Releasing the outer double negation turns ¬(P∨Q) into the full product ¬P∧¬Q; the ¬P conjunct is then projected as the exact antecedent consumed by ¬P→R.
puzzles.defeats: Recognizing De Morgan is not the endpoint: the produced connective is a product, and one exact product component must feed the later implication consumer while the other remains visible context.

puzzles.visibleSituation: The De Morgan alternatives ¬P and ¬Q feed separate case handlers to R; their common R result must then pass through R→S to meet a denied S.
puzzles.defeats: The produced alternatives cannot be treated as a product or as an endpoint: each enters its matching case consumer and the common result must continue downstream.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A cut-delimited P source and a P inside an incomparable deeper field are not initially in a usable copy direction. Wrapping exactly the deeper P creates a descendant cut matching the matrix-level source; Q or P+Q produces the wrong structure.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats net-polarity and proximity guesses: source and target P do not justify either removal before the new double-cut ancestry exists.

puzzles.visibleSituation: The complete P-Q double cut is the target. The matrix rejects insertion, while the existing positive cuts place a copy inside or beside the target and give it no ancestor authority. Wrapping the target creates a useful positive ancestor annulus where inserting the exact P-Q double cut enables its discharge.
puzzles.defeats: It defeats permissive-host and partial-shape guesses: the matrix and inner annulus have the wrong polarity, the pre-existing positive cuts have the wrong ancestry, and P alone cannot discharge the complete double cut.

puzzles.visibleSituation: A sole P occurrence looks erasable, but it is the only ancestor source for both P→Q and P→R before Q and R jointly meet the denied conjunction.
puzzles.defeats: Locally legal simplification is not globally harmless: erasing P removes the exact authority required by two separate downstream branches.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 4 semantic owners, 32 owned atomic occurrences, and 30 cuts. Factoring or distribution in a conjunction field reveals an absorption or idempotence redex.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the strategy of searching for an absorption or idempotence redex only in the original shape; conjunction-field reshaping exposes it.

puzzles.visibleSituation: Closed wire-free start with 5 semantic owners, 22 owned atomic occurrences, and 34 cuts. Factoring or distribution in a disjunction field reveals an absorption or idempotence redex.
puzzles.defeats: It overturns the strategy of searching for an absorption or idempotence redex only in the original shape; disjunction-field reshaping exposes it.

puzzles.visibleSituation: The whole context K=U∧V is supplied outside P∨Q, the branches map to R and S, and K must then be recollected with each mapped result by the downstream consumers.
puzzles.defeats: Mapping the branches does not finish their preparation; the exact whole shared K must be matched again after the branch-local result appears.
